mapsDirections.question: What is a map?
options: A drawing that shows what a place looks like from above
mapsDirections.fact: A map is a flat picture showing what an area looks like from above — like a bird's eye view!

mapsDirections.question: What direction does the sun rise?
mapsDirections.fact: The sun always rises in the East and sets in the West — these are key compass directions!

mapsDirections.question: How many main compass directions are there?
mapsDirections.fact: There are 4 main compass directions: North, South, East and West — remember NEWS!

mapsDirections.question: What is a compass used for?
options: To find North, South, East and West
mapsDirections.fact: A compass always points North — it helps explorers and travellers find their direction!

mapsDirections.question: What is a map key (legend) for?
options: To explain what the symbols on a map mean
mapsDirections.fact: A map key explains what each symbol means — a little tree might mean a forest, a dot might mean a town!

mapsDirections.question: If you face North, which direction is behind you?
mapsDirections.fact: If you face North, South is directly behind you! North and South are opposite directions.

mapsDirections.question: What does a scale on a map tell us?
options: How far apart places really are
mapsDirections.fact: A map scale shows real distances — for example, 1 cm on the map might equal 1 km in real life!

mapsDirections.question: Which of these would you find on a well-made map?
mapsDirections.fact: A good map has a compass rose, scale, key and a title to help you understand it!

mapsDirections.question: If East is on your right as you face North, what is on your left?
mapsDirections.fact: If East is right and you face North, then West is to your left — opposite to East!

mapsDirections.question: What is a globe?
options: A flat map of a country
A round model of the Earth
mapsDirections.fact: A globe is a round 3D model of Earth — it shows the true shape of continents and oceans!

mapsDirections.question: What symbol usually marks a city on a map?
mapsDirections.fact: Cities are usually shown with a dot or circle on maps — bigger cities sometimes have larger dots!

mapsDirections.question: Which way does a compass needle always point?
mapsDirections.fact: A compass needle always points towards magnetic North — it is how people have navigated for centuries!

mapsDirections.question: What is a street map used for?
options: To find your way around a town or city
mapsDirections.fact: Street maps show roads, buildings and landmarks to help you navigate around a town!

mapsDirections.question: What does NE stand for on a compass?
mapsDirections.fact: NE stands for North East — it is the direction between North and East on a compass!

mapsDirections.question: Which type of map shows where countries are in the world?
mapsDirections.fact: A world map shows all the countries and continents on Earth — great for geography!

mapsDirections.question: What is a landmark?
options: A well-known feature used for navigation
mapsDirections.fact: Landmarks are famous or easily recognised places — like a tall tower — used to help find your way!

mapsDirections.question: What are continents?
options: The largest areas of land on Earth
mapsDirections.fact: Continents are the seven huge landmasses on Earth: Africa, Asia, Europe, the Americas, Oceania, Antarctica!

mapsDirections.question: What is the equator?
options: An imaginary line around the middle of the Earth
mapsDirections.fact: The equator is an imaginary line around Earth's middle — countries near it are hottest because the sun is strongest there!

communityHelpers.question: Who helps us when our house is on fire?
communityHelpers.fact: Firefighters put out fires and rescue people — they are true heroes in our community!

communityHelpers.question: Who helps keep our community safe from crime?
communityHelpers.fact: Police officers protect people and keep order in our communities — they help in emergencies!

communityHelpers.question: Who teaches children at school?
communityHelpers.fact: Teachers help children learn and grow every day — one of the most important community helpers!

communityHelpers.question: Who helps us when we are sick or injured?
communityHelpers.fact: Doctors and nurses care for sick and injured people — they work in hospitals, clinics and surgeries!

communityHelpers.question: Who delivers letters and parcels to our homes?
communityHelpers.fact: Postal workers deliver our mail every day — even in rain and snow!

communityHelpers.question: Who grows the food we eat?
communityHelpers.fact: Farmers grow crops and raise animals for food — without farmers we would have nothing to eat!

communityHelpers.question: Who builds houses and buildings?
communityHelpers.fact: Construction workers and builders use tools and materials to build the homes and buildings we live in!

communityHelpers.question: Who flies aeroplanes?
communityHelpers.fact: Pilots fly aeroplanes — they train for many years to learn to fly safely through the skies!

communityHelpers.question: Who looks after our teeth?
communityHelpers.fact: Dentists check and care for our teeth — visiting the dentist regularly keeps your teeth healthy!

communityHelpers.question: Who looks after sick animals?
communityHelpers.fact: Vets (veterinarians) are doctors for animals — they treat pets and farm animals!

communityHelpers.question: Who helps us find books at a library?
communityHelpers.fact: Librarians help people find books and information — they run libraries which are open to everyone!

communityHelpers.question: Who prepares and cooks food in a restaurant?
communityHelpers.fact: Chefs are trained cooks who prepare delicious meals — they work in restaurants, hotels and schools!

communityHelpers.question: Who helps put out fires in the forest?
communityHelpers.fact: Firefighters tackle all types of fires — including huge forest fires that can spread very quickly!

communityHelpers.question: Who drives buses and trains?
communityHelpers.fact: Bus and train drivers take people where they need to go — public transport helps communities move!

communityHelpers.question: What do community helpers have in common?
options: They all earn lots of money
communityHelpers.fact: Community helpers all work to serve and help other people — their jobs make our community better!

communityHelpers.question: Who fixes broken pipes and water problems in homes?
communityHelpers.fact: Plumbers fix water pipes, taps and drains — they make sure we have clean water in our homes!

communityHelpers.question: Who helps at the scene of a car accident?
communityHelpers.fact: Paramedics and police officers both respond to accidents — teamwork saves lives!

communityHelpers.question: Why is it important to have community helpers?
options: So we don't have to work ourselves
To keep our community safe, healthy and running smoothly
So children don't have to go to school
communityHelpers.fact: Community helpers are essential — they keep us safe, healthy and educated, and make society function!

worldContinents.question: How many continents are there in the world?
worldContinents.fact: There are 7 continents: Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, South America, Oceania and Antarctica!

worldContinents.question: Which is the largest continent?
worldContinents.fact: Asia is the largest continent — it covers about 30% of Earth's total land area!

worldContinents.question: Which continent is at the South Pole?
worldContinents.fact: Antarctica is at the South Pole — it is the coldest, windiest and driest continent on Earth!

worldContinents.question: Which continent has the most countries?
worldContinents.fact: Africa has 54 countries — more than any other continent! It is also the second largest continent.

worldContinents.question: Which continent is Indonesia in?
worldContinents.fact: Indonesia is in Asia — it is the largest archipelago (group of islands) country in the world!

worldContinents.question: Which ocean lies between Europe and North America?
worldContinents.fact: The Atlantic Ocean separates Europe and North America — it is the second largest ocean!

worldContinents.question: What is the smallest continent?
worldContinents.fact: Oceania (also called Australia) is the smallest continent — it includes Australia, New Zealand and Pacific islands!

worldContinents.question: Which continent is Egypt in?
worldContinents.fact: Egypt is in Africa — it is famous for its ancient pyramids and the Nile River!

worldContinents.question: Which continent is Brazil in?
worldContinents.fact: Brazil is in South America — it has the Amazon Rainforest, the world's largest tropical rainforest!

worldContinents.question: What are the five oceans of the world?
worldContinents.fact: The 5 oceans are Atlantic, Pacific, Indian, Arctic and Southern — they cover 71% of Earth's surface!

worldContinents.question: Which continent is France in?
worldContinents.fact: France is in Europe — Europe is home to 44 countries including the UK, Germany and Spain!

worldContinents.question: Which continent is Canada in?
worldContinents.fact: Canada is in North America — it is the second largest country in the world by area!

worldContinents.question: Which is the least populated continent?
worldContinents.fact: Antarctica has no permanent residents — only scientists live there temporarily in research stations!

worldContinents.question: What is the largest country in the world?
worldContinents.fact: Russia is the largest country in the world — it spans 11 time zones across Europe and Asia!

worldContinents.question: Which continent has the Amazon Rainforest?
worldContinents.fact: The Amazon Rainforest is in South America — it produces 20% of the world's oxygen!

worldContinents.question: On which continent would you find the Sahara Desert?
worldContinents.fact: The Sahara Desert is in Africa — it is the largest hot desert in the world!

worldContinents.question: Which ocean is the largest?
worldContinents.fact: The Pacific Ocean is the largest — it covers more area than all Earth's land put together!

worldContinents.question: What do we call people who study countries and continents?
worldContinents.fact: Geographers study the Earth — including continents, countries, weather, people and environments!

needsWants.question: What is a 'need'?
options: Something we would like to have
Something we must have to survive
needsWants.fact: A need is something we cannot live without — like food, water, shelter and clothing!

needsWants.question: Which of these is a NEED?
needsWants.fact: Food is a need — without food, our bodies cannot grow or function properly!

needsWants.question: What is a 'want'?
options: Something we must have to live
Something we would like but do not need to survive
needsWants.fact: A want is something nice to have but not essential — like toys, games or sweets!

needsWants.question: Which of these is a WANT?
needsWants.fact: A video game is a want — it is fun but we can survive without it!

needsWants.question: Which of these is a NEED?
needsWants.fact: Shelter is a need — we need protection from cold, heat and rain to stay healthy and safe!

needsWants.question: Why is it important to know the difference between needs and wants?
options: So we can buy everything we want
To help us make good decisions about money
So we never have to work
needsWants.fact: Understanding needs vs wants helps us make smart choices — meet needs before spending on wants!

needsWants.question: Which of these is a NEED?
needsWants.fact: Clothing is a basic need — we need it to protect us from the weather and stay healthy!

needsWants.question: A child asks for a toy but the family needs groceries. Which should come first?
needsWants.fact: Groceries (food) are a need — needs should always come before wants when making choices!

needsWants.question: Which of these is a WANT?
needsWants.fact: Jewellery is a want — it is decorative but not necessary for survival!

needsWants.question: What is a budget?
options: A plan for how to spend money
needsWants.fact: A budget is a plan for spending money wisely — it helps make sure needs are covered before wants!

needsWants.question: Which of these is a NEED?
needsWants.fact: Medicine is a need when you are sick — keeping healthy is essential for life!

needsWants.question: What might happen if a family spends all their money on wants?
options: They might not be able to afford their needs
needsWants.fact: If we spend on wants first, we might not have enough for needs — this is why budgeting matters!

needsWants.question: Which of these is a WANT?
needsWants.fact: Ice cream is a want — it is delicious but not essential for staying alive!

needsWants.question: Is clean water a need or a want?
needsWants.fact: Clean water is an absolute need — our bodies are about 60% water and we cannot survive without it!

needsWants.question: Which of these is a NEED for a baby?
needsWants.fact: A baby needs milk and food to grow — this is the most important need for a young child!

needsWants.question: Can the same thing be a need for one person and a want for another?
needsWants.fact: Context matters — a wheelchair is a need for someone who cannot walk, but might be a want in other situations!

needsWants.question: Which of these is a WANT?
needsWants.fact: A funfair trip is a want — it is a fun experience but not essential for survival!

needsWants.question: What is the most important thing to buy FIRST when you have limited money?
needsWants.fact: Needs always come first — food, shelter, water and healthcare are more important than any want!

holidaysCultures.question: Which festival celebrates the new year in China?
holidaysCultures.fact: Lunar New Year (Chinese New Year) is celebrated with fireworks, red envelopes and dragon dances!

holidaysCultures.question: What is Diwali known as?
holidaysCultures.fact: Diwali is the Festival of Lights — celebrated by Hindus, Sikhs and Jains with lamps, fireworks and sweets!

holidaysCultures.question: Which holiday involves giving gifts and decorating a tree?
holidaysCultures.fact: Christmas is celebrated on 25 December — many people decorate trees and exchange gifts!

holidaysCultures.question: What is Eid al-Fitr?
options: A celebration at the end of Ramadan for Muslims
holidaysCultures.fact: Eid al-Fitr marks the end of Ramadan (the Muslim month of fasting) — families feast and give gifts!

holidaysCultures.question: What is the name of the Jewish festival of lights?
holidaysCultures.fact: Hanukkah is the Jewish Festival of Lights — celebrated for 8 nights by lighting a menorah candle each night!

holidaysCultures.question: Which festival involves throwing coloured powder?
holidaysCultures.fact: Holi is a Hindu festival of colours — people throw bright coloured powder to celebrate spring!

holidaysCultures.question: What is culture?
options: The beliefs, traditions and way of life shared by a group of people
holidaysCultures.fact: Culture includes a group's food, language, music, traditions, art and celebrations — it makes each community unique!

holidaysCultures.question: What food is traditionally eaten at Chinese New Year for good luck?
holidaysCultures.fact: Dumplings are eaten at Chinese New Year — their shape resembles ancient gold ingots, symbolising wealth and luck!

holidaysCultures.question: Which country celebrates Thanksgiving as a national holiday?
holidaysCultures.fact: Thanksgiving is celebrated in the USA on the fourth Thursday of November — families give thanks and eat a big meal!

holidaysCultures.question: What does the word 'traditional' mean?
holidaysCultures.fact: Traditional means something handed down from one generation to the next — like recipes, clothes and celebrations!

holidaysCultures.question: Why is it important to learn about different cultures?
options: To understand, respect and celebrate how different people live
holidaysCultures.fact: Learning about other cultures builds respect and understanding — it helps us live together peacefully in a diverse world!

holidaysCultures.question: What is a tradition?
options: A custom or practice passed down through time
holidaysCultures.fact: A tradition is something done repeatedly over time — like a family always making a special meal on birthdays!

holidaysCultures.question: Which festival is celebrated by decorating homes with lanterns?
holidaysCultures.fact: Diwali homes are decorated with diyas (clay lamps) and lanterns — fireworks light up the night sky!

holidaysCultures.question: What do Muslims do during Ramadan?
options: Fast from sunrise to sunset each day
holidaysCultures.fact: During Ramadan, Muslims fast from sunrise to sunset for 30 days — it is a time for prayer and reflection!

holidaysCultures.question: Which festival involves wearing costumes and trick-or-treating?
holidaysCultures.fact: Halloween is celebrated on 31 October — children dress up in costumes and go trick-or-treating!

holidaysCultures.question: What is a national holiday?
options: A holiday you take at school
A day off celebrated across a whole country
holidaysCultures.fact: A national holiday is a day celebrated by a whole country — like Independence Day in the USA!

holidaysCultures.question: What is the Islamic greeting used during Eid?
holidaysCultures.fact: 'Eid Mubarak' means 'Blessed Eid' in Arabic — it is the traditional greeting shared during Eid celebrations!

holidaysCultures.question: Why do different countries have different celebrations?
options: Because they do not talk to each other
Each country has its own unique history, religion and traditions
Because they celebrate on different days by chance
holidaysCultures.fact: Celebrations reflect a country's history, religion and culture — diversity in celebrations makes the world interesting!

timelinesHistory.question: What is a timeline?
options: A drawing that shows events in the order they happened
timelinesHistory.fact: A timeline shows events in the order they happened — it helps us see how the past led to the present!

timelinesHistory.question: What does 'past' mean?
options: What will happen in the future
timelinesHistory.fact: The past is everything that has already happened — history studies the past to learn from it!

timelinesHistory.question: What does 'present' mean in time?
timelinesHistory.fact: The present is right now — this exact moment! It is between the past and the future.

timelinesHistory.question: What does 'future' mean?
timelinesHistory.fact: The future is what has not happened yet — it is everything still to come after right now!

timelinesHistory.question: What is history?
options: The study of the past — events and people from long ago
timelinesHistory.fact: History is the study of past events — we learn from history to understand how the world became what it is today!

timelinesHistory.question: What is a source in history?
options: Evidence from the past, like a document or object
timelinesHistory.fact: Historical sources are evidence — diaries, photographs, buildings and objects all help us learn about the past!

timelinesHistory.question: Which of these is a PRIMARY source?
options: A diary written by someone at the time
timelinesHistory.fact: A primary source was created at the time of the event — like diaries, letters, photos or objects from that period!

timelinesHistory.question: What do we call objects or remains from the past?
timelinesHistory.fact: Artefacts are objects made or used by people in the past — archaeologists dig them up and study them!

timelinesHistory.question: Why do we study history?
options: To understand how we got to where we are and learn from the past
timelinesHistory.fact: Studying history helps us understand our world, learn from mistakes and appreciate how people lived before us!

timelinesHistory.question: What does BC mean in history?
options: Before Christ — counting years before year 1
timelinesHistory.fact: BC means 'Before Christ' — years counted backwards from year 1. The larger the BC number, the further back in time!

timelinesHistory.question: What does AD mean in dates?
options: Anno Domini — years counted from year 1 onwards
timelinesHistory.fact: AD stands for 'Anno Domini' (Latin for 'in the year of the Lord') — the years we use today, like 2025 AD!

timelinesHistory.question: What is an archaeologist?
options: A person who digs up and studies objects from the past
timelinesHistory.fact: Archaeologists excavate (dig up) sites to find artefacts and learn about how people lived long ago!

timelinesHistory.question: What is a museum?
options: A place where historical objects and art are kept and displayed
timelinesHistory.fact: Museums collect, preserve and display objects from history and art — visiting them is like stepping into the past!

timelinesHistory.question: On a timeline, which event comes first?
options: The one furthest to the right
The one furthest to the left
timelinesHistory.fact: On a left-to-right timeline, events on the left happened first — earliest events are always on the left!

timelinesHistory.question: What is a century?
timelinesHistory.fact: A century is 100 years — we are currently living in the 21st century (the 2000s)!

timelinesHistory.question: What is a decade?
timelinesHistory.fact: A decade is 10 years — the 1990s was a decade, and so was the 2010s!

timelinesHistory.question: What is a millennium?
timelinesHistory.fact: A millennium is 1000 years — we celebrated a new millennium in the year 2000!

timelinesHistory.question: Which of these is a secondary source?
options: A letter written during a war
A photograph taken at the time of an event
A history book written about an event by a modern author
timelinesHistory.fact: A secondary source was created after the event, using primary sources — like a history textbook written today about a past war!
